free_text: Study of structure in the 28Si(p,n)28P and
28Si(p,alpha)25Al excitation functions
OG
A large surface-area SiO2 target, approximately
0.9 mg/cm2.
A plastic scintillator was used to observe the
decay of 28P via the 11.25 MeV beta+. A thin aluminum
window stopped the protons, the high-energy positrons
to reach the detector, placed at 90 degrees to the
incident beam.
The background yield was subtracted.
No information on uncertainties.
Fig.1 (p.177) in Nucl.Phys.A,232,176,1974 was
digitized by GSYS2.4.7.
Thin SiO2, targets, 50-100 mu-g/cm2, were used and
placed in a large volume scattering chamber.
Six surface barrier detectors (100 mu-m) spaced
10 degrees apart.
Impurity peaks due to the 29Si and 30Si in the natural
targets contributed to the under-lying background, and
contributed to the uncertainty in the background
subtraction. Proton background and dead time
corrections were made.
The largest source of error was due to the background
subtraction and the statistical errors.
A combination of background subtraction,
statistical, beam integration, and relative solid angle
determination errors gives a relative uncertainty in
the data which varies between 5 and 15 %.
The absolute error is 15 % and arises from the
uncertainty in the determination of the target
thickness-solid angle product.
Background subtraction errors (about 5%)
Statistical errors vary from 2% to 15%.
Figs.3-5 in Nucl.Phys.A,232,176,1974 was
digitized by GSYS2.4.7.

Excitation function
Thin SiO2, targets, 50-100 mu-g/cm2, were used and
placed in a large volume scattering chamber.
Six surface barrier detectors (100 mu-m) spaced
10 degrees apart.
Impurity peaks due to the 29Si and 30Si in the natural
targets contributed to the under-lying background, and
contributed to the uncertainty in the background
subtraction. Proton background and dead time
corrections were made.
No information on uncertainties.
Hauser-Feshbach and distorted-wave Born
approximation (DWBA) calculations.
Fig.7 (p.185) in Nucl.Phys.A,232,176,1974 was
digitized by GSYS2.4.7.
